rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE TREVOR KNOOP FIRST PARTY SPECIAL NEEDS TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Records (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 2] In any event, the litigation parties' interests do not override the public interest in open court records in these minor's compromise and special needs trust proceedings that are intended to be robust to ensure that the beneficiary's interests are protected. Albert's reliance on the beneficiary's "further psychological and emotional harm, embarrassment and humiliation" is similarly misplaced. (See MPA at 3.) Neither the Petition to Establish SNT nor the settlements discuss any details of the beneficiary's "physiological, medical, and emotional status and treatment." There is no showing as to how not sealing the fact of settlements, the amounts or the amount of bond would lead to "further air[ing] and reliv[ing of] those highly personal and sensitive matters" or lead to "further psychological and emotional harm, embarrassment and humiliation." In any event, the court finds that having the information at issue in the public record is not likely to lead to the claimed harm. Nor has Albert presented specific facts establishing a substantial probability that the alleged overriding interests will be prejudiced if the requested sealing is not approved. (See Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(d)(3).) As already noted, the materials before the court disclose nothing material about the beneficiary's "physiological, medical, and emotional" information. Nor would granting Albert's motion relieve any harm from such disclosures, given that she seeks to seal information--the fact of settlements, the amounts funding the trust and the amount of bond-entirely unrelated to the beneficiary's condition, injuries and treatment. In any event any harm in this area from the absence of sealing has already occurred, given that each of the four versions of the underlying civil complaint discuss, in detail, allegations of the injurious and negligent actions by defendants and the plaintiff's resulting injuries and disability. No sealing order here can change this reality. Albert shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a hard-copy proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling prior to the hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on May 26, 2021. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925#, participant ID 0#, and passcode 722809#. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE TREVOR KNOOP FIRST PARTY SPECIAL NEEDS TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Records
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. Guardian Ad Litem Laura Albert's Motion to Seal Records is DENIED. A court may order that a record be filed under seal only if it expressly finds facts that establish: (1) an overriding interest exists that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) the overriding interest supports sealing the records; (3) a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and, (5) no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(d)(1)-(5); NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV), Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (Locke) (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178, 1216-1218 ("NBC Subsidiary").) The party seeking the sealing of a record bears the burden of presenting information sufficient to identify the specific information claimed to be entitled to such treatment and the nature of the harm threatened by disclosure. (H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 894.) The moving party must set forth specific facts in support of a motion to seal; statements that are too conclusory, abstract, or generic are not sufficient to satisfy the burden. (Id. at pp. 895-896.) The public's right to access to court records is fundamental to our constitutional system. (See NBC Subsidiary, supra, 20 Cal.4th 1216-1217 [First Amendment principles require that substantive courtroom proceedings in ordinary civil cases are "presumptively open"]; Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(c) [court records presumed to be open].) The right to access is heightened in probate proceedings where broad notice and opportunity to object is fundamental. (See, e.g., Estate of Stevenson (2006) 141 Cal.App.4th 1074, 1087-88 ["Notice is the cornerstone of estate proceedings. [Citations.]"].) Notice is lacking when the document submitted for judicial approval is withheld from interested parties, effectively removing them from the decision-making process. Specifically, the court's consideration of Albert's Petition to Establish Special Needs Trust and Approve Attorney Fees and Costs will require an open discussion of the information, including the beneficiary's needs, the amount to be paid to the trust and the amount of bond. (See Prob. C., section 3604, subd. (b).) An open discussion assumes access to the record. Additionally, any future proceedings relative to the special needs trust will likely require open discussion of this information, which will not be possible if the information is under sealed. (See Prob. C., sections 3602, 3604(a), 3611; see also Cal. Rules of Ct., rules 6.903(c), 7.903.) Albert has not presented specific facts showing that the litigation parties' or the beneficiary's privacy interests outweigh the public interest. (See Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(d)(2).) She seeks to seal the fact of settlements, the amounts funding the trust and the amount of bond. Her reliance on the litigation parties' agreement to keep the settlements confidential is misplaced, given that the settlements expressly except from confidentiality court filings in certain proceedings (see, Exhibit A Settlement, at page 6 of 12, and page 7 of 12; Exhibit B Settlement, at page 4, paragraph 16; Exhibit C Settlement, at page 6 of 11). The court finds the establishment of the proposed Knoop SNT is a "necessary court proceeding to obtain approval of minor's compromise or good faith settlement determination" in the underlying civil case. (See Prob. C., section 2504, 3500, 3600 et seq.; Code Civ. Proc., section 372; San Francisco Local Rules, rule 6.10.) The filings are necessary to establish the trust and secure approval of the minor's compromise petition and, thus, are plainly covered by the confidentiality-exception language. [Part 1 of 2]
